title: Lamb saag
subtitle: Slow-braised lamb in spiced spinach sauce
excerpt: Lamb shoulder pieces braised until tender in a silky, spiced spinach sauce. The spinach is blended smooth after wilting to give the sauce its distinctive deep-green colour and coating consistency. The lamb and spinach combination produces a dish with more substance than saag paneer and more depth than a plain lamb curry — a slow-cooked main that benefits from making a day ahead.
sourceNotes: Saag gosht (lamb in spinach) is a Punjabi dish appearing in Madhur Jaffrey's cookbooks and widely on British Indian restaurant menus. The combination of lamb and leafy greens is fundamental to Punjabi cooking; the British curry-house version uses spinach blended to a smooth sauce.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make 2 days ahead. The spinach sauce deepens overnight.

content.text: Lamb saag requires two long stages: the lamb must be browned and the onion base must be properly caramelised before the spinach is added, and then the whole dish must simmer for at least 75 minutes for the lamb to become tender. Don't attempt to rush either stage — the browned lamb and caramelised onion base are what give the finished dish its depth.

The spinach is added in its raw (or barely wilted) state and then blended with the onion and spice base into a smooth green paste that forms the braising liquid. This is different from the saag paneer technique where the spinach is wilted separately — here the raw spinach is blended into the base before braising, which gives the sauce a more integrated flavour.
Brown the lamb and make the spinach base

Heat the {{ghee}} in a large heavy pan over high heat. Brown the {{lamb-shoulder}} in batches for 3 minutes per batch. Remove and set aside. Reduce heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 12 minutes until deep golden. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{chilli-green}}, {{turmeric}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, and {{chilli-powder}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{spinach}} in batches, wilting each addition before adding the next. Once all the spinach has wilted, blend the entire mixture until smooth using a stick blender.
Braise the lamb in the spinach sauce

Return the browned lamb to the pan with the blended spinach sauce. Add 150 ml water and bring to a simmer. Cover and cook on the lowest heat for 75 minutes, stirring every 20 minutes, until the lamb is tender and the sauce has thickened around it. Stir in the {{garam-masala}}, add the {{double-cream}} if using, and season with salt.

items.symptom: Sauce khaki and dull rather than dark green
items.cause: Spinach overcooked during the wilting stage
items.fix: Wilt the spinach only until soft before blending — do not cook further. The green colour comes from chlorophyll, which degrades with extended heat

items.cause: Temperature too low or shoulder pieces too large
items.fix: Ensure the sauce is simmering, not just warm. Add water if the pan runs dry and continue for up to 2 hours total

items.cause: Spinach ratio too high relative to liquid
items.fix: Add hot water 50 ml at a time and stir until the desired consistency is reached — it should be pourable but coating

items.symptom: Spinach flavour too raw and vegetal
items.fix: Let the spinach wilt properly in the pan before blending — 3 to 4 minutes per batch. Raw spinach blended into the sauce tastes sharp and green rather than rich

content.text: Lamb saag is a complete main course — the spinach provides iron and greenness; the lamb provides richness and protein; the two together have a weight and depth that makes this one of the more satisfying dishes on the British Indian menu. It sits alongside rogan josh and dhansak as a slow-cooked lamb curry for diners who want substance. The dish improves overnight as the lamb continues to absorb the spiced spinach sauce, which is one reason it appears frequently at curry nights and large gatherings where it can be made the day before.
